title: Rubric Atomicity AND Criteria
reminderTitle: One criterion = one thing. AND = split.
reminderBody:
The most common rubric rejection: bundling two independent checks into one criterion with AND. If a solution could pass one part and fail the other, they must be separate criteria. QC can't give half a point each criterion is all-or-nothing.

Watch for: commas, "and", "as well as", "also" connecting two distinct behaviors in one criterion.
badExample:
// ❌ Two independent checks in one criterion
"The solution increments the like counter AND removes the like button after clicking"
quickRule: If your criterion has AND, split it into two.

title: Rubric Self-Containment No 'Existing Behavior'
reminderTitle: A grader can't read the codebase. Embed every expected value.
reminderBody:
A rubric criterion must be gradable using only the criterion text, the full rubric, and the model's response. The grader cannot open the repo, check the original UI, or read your prompt.

Red flag words: "original", "existing", "previous", "unchanged", "preserved", "same as before", "current behavior". Any of these without a concrete value = instant self-containment failure.

Fix: replace the relative reference with the actual expected value.
badExample:
// ❌ Grader doesn't know what 'existing behavior' means
"The existing cart total calculation behavior is preserved"
goodExample:
// ✅ Concrete expected value embedded in the criterion
"CartComponent.total() returns the sum of (item.price × item.quantity) for all items in the cart"
quickRule: If your criterion requires the grader to check the codebase to know what 'existing' means, rewrite it.

title: Rubric Granularity Don't Introduce Method Names
reminderTitle: Your rubric must stay as abstract as your prompt.
reminderBody:
If your prompt says "add a dark mode toggle", your rubric must check observable behavior not whether a specific method called toggleDarkMode() exists. The rubric must not be more specific than the prompt.

A rubric criterion that names a method, class, file, or variable NOT mentioned in the prompt is a granularity failure. It also creates gold-patch coupling: only your implementation passes, not any valid alternative.

Rule of thumb: if a completely different valid implementation would fail your rubric criterion, it's too specific.
badExample:
// ❌ Method name not in prompt → gold-patch coupling
"themeService.toggleDarkMode() is called when the toggle is clicked"
goodExample:
// ✅ Observable behavior any valid implementation passes
"Clicking the dark mode toggle switches the page color scheme between light and dark"
quickRule: If a method, class, or file isn't in the prompt, it can't be in the rubric.

title: Rubric Language Banned Words
reminderBody:
Vague words make objective grading impossible. If two graders would disagree on whether the criterion passes, it's not specific enough.

Banned words in rubric criteria: properly, correctly, appropriately, reasonable, clean, good, well, efficient, adequate, suitable, best practices.

Every criterion must describe a concrete, observable pass/fail condition. Replace the vague word with the specific behavior you're checking.
badExample:
// ❌ 'Correctly' tells the grader nothing
"The checkout form correctly handles validation errors"
goodExample:
// ✅ Concrete observable outcome
"Submitting the checkout form with an empty email field displays the text 'Email is required' below the email input"
quickRule: Replace any vague word with the concrete observable outcome.

title: Rubric Coverage Every Requirement Needs a Criterion
reminderTitle: A missing rubric criterion is a free pass for the model to skip that requirement.
reminderBody:
Every explicit requirement in your prompt must have a corresponding rubric criterion. If the prompt asks for three behaviors and the rubric only checks two, the model can ignore the third and still pass.

How to check: read the prompt, list every distinct requirement (R1, R2, R3...), then verify each one has a rubric criterion. Also check the opposite: every rubric criterion must trace back to something in the prompt criteria checking things the prompt never asked for are orphans.

Remember: f2p_success and p2p_success are mandatory and always count toward the required weight.
badExample:
Prompt requires: (1) dark mode toggle, (2) localStorage persistence, (3) system preference detection

Rubric only covers:
"toggle switches theme" ✅
"preference saved to localStorage" ✅
// Missing: system preference detection ← model can skip this entirely
goodExample:
Prompt requires: (1) dark mode toggle, (2) localStorage persistence, (3) system preference detection

Rubric covers all three:
"toggle switches theme" ✅
"preference saved to localStorage" ✅
"on first load, theme matches prefers-color-scheme" ✅
quickRule: List every prompt requirement. Verify each has a rubric criterion. No gaps allowed.

title: F2P Tests What Makes a Valid F2P
reminderTitle: F2P = Fail in Phase 1, Pass in Phase 2. For the right reason.
reminderBody:
An F2P test must:
1. Fail before the gold patch is applied (Phase 1)
2. Pass after the gold patch is applied (Phase 2)
3. Be writable from the prompt alone without reading the gold patch
4. Test observable behavior: return values, UI output, API responses

The most common F2P mistake: writing a test that passes in Phase 1 because the assertion is too loose, or fails for the wrong reason (compile error instead of behavioral failure).

Key question: "Could I write this test knowing only the prompt?" If you needed to read the gold patch, it's likely coupled.
quickRule: If your F2P test passes before the gold patch, it's not an F2P test.

title: Tests Coupled to the Gold Patch
reminderTitle: Tests must pass for ANY valid solution, not just yours.
reminderBody:
Gold-patch coupling happens when an F2P test references a specific method, class, or property that exists in YOUR implementation but is not mentioned in the prompt.

The problem: a different developer could build a perfectly correct solution using different method names, and their solution would fail your tests. QC rejects this because the test is measuring implementation, not behavior.

How to spot it: look at every method call in your tests. Is that method name in the prompt? If not, it's coupled.

Fix: test the observable output what the user sees or what the API returns not the internal implementation.
quickRule: If the method name isn't in the prompt, don't call it in a test.

title: P2P Tests Guard Existing Behavior
reminderTitle: P2P tests verify existing functionality wasn't broken by your changes.
reminderBody:
P2P (Pass-to-Pass) tests must pass in BOTH phases: before the gold patch and after. They guard pre-existing behavior that your gold patch might accidentally break.

Good P2P targets:
- Files your gold patch modifies (regression risk)
- Features adjacent to what you changed
- Core functionality unrelated to the new feature

Bad P2P tests:
- Test the exact behavior the bug introduces (passes because of the bug)
- Pass trivially regardless of code state (e.g., clearCart always returns 0)

Rule: for every file your gold patch touches, ask "what existing behavior could I break?" and write a P2P test for it.
quickRule: P2P tests must pass before AND after the gold patch. Test real regressions, not trivialities.

title: Static Tests Tests Must Run Code, Not Read It
reminderTitle: grep-style tests that inspect source code are always rejected.
reminderBody:
A static test reads the source code of the repository instead of executing it. Common forms:
- fs.readFileSync() to check if a string appears in a file
- Regex on source code content
- Checking if a module exports a specific name
- Inspecting package.json for a dependency

These tests pass or fail based on code structure, not behavior. They break if someone refactors without changing behavior.

Every test must execute the code and assert on an observable result.
quickRule: Tests must run the code, not read it. No grep, no readFileSync, no regex on source files.

title: Write Prompts Like a Developer, Not a Spec Sheet
reminderTitle: One problem, one ask. No bullet lists, no step-by-step.
reminderBody:
Your prompt should read like a GitHub issue written by a senior developer to a colleague. Short, direct, describing the problem or missing feature not a numbered spec with every implementation detail spelled out.

Red flags:
- Numbered requirement lists
- Step-by-step instructions ("First open file.ts, then...")
- Mentioning exact file/method/class names the model should create
- LLM-style formatting: bullet points, "Do not modify X", "Ensure that Y"

If your prompt looks like a homework assignment, rewrite it.
badExample:
// ❌ Spec sheet model passes by transcription, not reasoning
Implement the following:
1. Create ThemeService in src/services/theme.service.ts
2. Add toggleDarkMode() method that updates body class
3. Add getTheme() method that reads localStorage
4. Register ThemeService in app.module.ts
5. Add a toggle button with id="theme-toggle"
goodExample:
// ✅ Developer voice model has to reason about implementation
The app has no dark mode. Add a user-controlled toggle in the header that switches between light and dark themes. The user's preference should persist across page reloads.
quickRule: Would a senior dev write this in a GitHub issue? If it looks like a homework assignment, rewrite it.

title: Specification Overload Too Many Requirements
reminderTitle: Too many explicit requirements = model passes by transcription, not reasoning.
reminderBody:
Specification overload happens when a prompt lists so many specific requirements that the model doesn't need to understand the codebase it just follows the recipe. This makes the benchmark useless.

Signs of spec overload:
- More than ~5 explicit requirements
- Naming exact files, methods, and wiring steps
- "Do not modify X", "Make sure Y is unchanged"
- The prompt reads like a diff description

Fix: describe the observable behavior the user would experience. Leave the implementation to the model.
badExample:
// ❌ 12 requirements model just follows the list
Add a search feature:
- Create SearchService in src/services/search.service.ts
- Add search() method filtering by name and category
- Add 300ms debounce in SearchComponent
- Use RxJS fromEvent on the input
- Emit results to a BehaviorSubject
- Subscribe in template with async pipe
- Add loading state during search
- Show 'No results found' when empty
- Highlight matching text
- Add keyboard navigation
- Write unit tests for SearchService
- Add error handling for failed requests
goodExample:
// ✅ 2 requirements model has to architect the solution
The product list has no search functionality. Add a search bar that filters visible products by name in real time. Results should update as the user types.
quickRule: If the model doesn't need to think to implement your prompt, it's over-specified.

title: Issue Type Mismatch Words Matter
reminderTitle: Bug Injection = fix. New Feature = add. Migration = migrate. Don't mix them.
reminderBody:
Each issue type has a specific framing. Using the wrong verbs is a Quick Fail in QC.

Issue type → correct framing:
- Bug Injection: describes a specific broken behavior to fix
- New Feature Request: describes missing functionality to add
- Migration: describes moving from one technology/version to another
- Performance Optimization: describes a measurable slowness or resource problem
- Maintenance/Testing Enhancement: describes code quality or test coverage improvement

The model fails differently depending on type. A New Feature prompt written with bug-fix language confuses both the model and QC.
quickRule: Bug fix language in a New Feature prompt = automatic rejection.

title: Vague Prompts Cause Coupled Tests
reminderTitle: If you can't write F2P tests without reading the gold patch, your prompt is too vague.
reminderBody:
There's a direct link between prompt vagueness and test quality. When a prompt is too vague, you don't know what behavior to test so you end up reading the gold patch to write tests. That creates coupling.

The test: after writing your prompt, close the gold patch and write your F2P tests using only the prompt. If you find yourself needing to check how you implemented it to know what to assert, the prompt isn't specific enough.

Specific enough means: you know what the UI shows, what the API returns, what error messages appear not HOW it's implemented, just WHAT it produces.
quickRule: Write your F2P tests before touching the gold patch. If you can't, the prompt needs more behavioral specificity.

title: ENTRYPOINT Goes in base.Dockerfile Only
reminderTitle: instance.Dockerfile must never define ENTRYPOINT or CMD.
reminderBody:
The template is strict about this: ENTRYPOINT belongs only in base.Dockerfile. The instance.Dockerfile extends the base image it does not redefine the entry point.

Why: the pipeline expects ENTRYPOINT to be set in the base. If instance.Dockerfile overrides it, container behavior changes unpredictably.

Also: CMD is equally prohibited in instance.Dockerfile.

Quick scan: search your instance.Dockerfile for ENTRYPOINT or CMD. If either appears, remove it.
badExample:
# instance.Dockerfile ❌
FROM stargazer-my-repo-base

RUN apt-get install -y some-dep

ENTRYPOINT ["/bin/bash"]   # ← WRONG: belongs only in base.Dockerfile
goodExample:
# instance.Dockerfile ✅
FROM stargazer-my-repo-base

# PROJECT DEPENDENCIES AND CONFIGURATION
RUN --mount=type=bind,source=patches/basetoinstance.patch,target=/tmp/basetoinstance.patch \
    if [ -s /tmp/basetoinstance.patch ]; then git apply --whitespace=fix /tmp/basetoinstance.patch; fi

# No ENTRYPOINT here it's defined in base.Dockerfile
quickRule: ENTRYPOINT in instance.Dockerfile = instant template violation.

title: base.Dockerfile Scope No Test Config Files
reminderTitle: base.Dockerfile clones the repo and installs deps. Nothing else.
reminderBody:
The base.Dockerfile has one job: set up the repo in a clean, runnable state. It must NOT:
- Create test config files (vitest.config.ts, jest.config.js, .babelrc)
- Inject environment variables for the app
- Rewrite package.json or any project files
- Install test frameworks that belong in the test patch

Think of base.Dockerfile as what a developer gets after a fresh git clone and npm install. Test setup belongs in the test patch, not the Docker image.

Also: always use full clone (no --depth flag) and include the PAT in the clone URL.
badExample:
# base.Dockerfile ❌
FROM node:20
WORKDIR /app
RUN git clone https://github.com/org/repo.git .

# Creating test config WRONG
RUN echo '{"test":{"environment":"jsdom"}}' > vitest.config.ts
RUN npm install --save-dev jest @testing-library/react

ENTRYPOINT ["/bin/bash"]
goodExample:
# base.Dockerfile ✅
FROM node:20
WORKDIR /app
RUN git clone https://$PAT@github.com/org/repo.git .
RUN git checkout $(git rev-list -n 1 HEAD)
RUN npm install

ENTRYPOINT ["/bin/bash"]
quickRule: If it's not in the official template, it doesn't belong in base.Dockerfile.

title: run_script.sh Apply Patches and Run Tests. That's It.
reminderTitle: run_script.sh has exactly two jobs. Everything else is wrong.
reminderBody:
run_script.sh responsibilities:
1. Apply patches (test_patch in Phase 1, gold_patch in Phase 2)
2. Run the test command

It must NOT: parse test results, format output, generate config files, validate the environment, or call parse_results.sh.

The template structure must be preserved: config variables at top, apply_patch() helper, Phase 1 section (=== PHASE 1 START ===), Phase 2 section (=== PHASE 2 START ===). The ONLY thing you customize is the test runner command.

Output must go to $RAW_OUTPUT_FILE via tee.
badExample:
# run_script.sh ❌ parsing results inside the script
apply_patch() { ... }

# Phase 1
apply_patch test_patch.patch
npx jest --json > /tmp/results-phase1.json
node -e "const r=require('/tmp/results-phase1.json'); ..."
goodExample:
# run_script.sh ✅ apply and run only
apply_patch() { ... }

echo '=== PHASE 1 START ===' | tee -a "$RAW_OUTPUT_FILE"
apply_patch patches/test_patch.patch
npx jest 2>&1 | tee -a "$RAW_OUTPUT_FILE"

echo '=== PHASE 2 START ===' | tee -a "$RAW_OUTPUT_FILE"
apply_patch patches/gold_patch.patch
npx jest 2>&1 | tee -a "$RAW_OUTPUT_FILE"
quickRule: run_script.sh applies patches and runs tests. Everything else is wrong.

title: parse_results.sh Pure Bash Only
reminderTitle: No node -e, no python, no external tools in parse_results.sh.
reminderBody:
parse_results.sh reads the raw output file from run_script.sh and produces test_results.json. It must do this in pure bash.

It must:
- Read $RAW_OUTPUT_FILE (the raw log from run_script.sh)
- Use phase markers (=== PHASE 1 START ===, === PHASE 2 START ===) to split phases
- Output test_results.json with phase_1, phase_2, and overall_result
- Use only bash built-ins, grep, awk, sed

Common violation: adding a Node.js snippet to parse JSON test output.
goodExample:
# parse_results.sh ✅ pure bash
#!/bin/bash
PHASE1=$(sed -n '/=== PHASE 1 START ===/,/=== PHASE 2 START ===/p' "$RAW_OUTPUT_FILE")
PHASE2=$(sed -n '/=== PHASE 2 START ===/,$p' "$RAW_OUTPUT_FILE")

P1_PASS=$(echo "$PHASE1" | grep -c 'PASS')
P1_FAIL=$(echo "$PHASE1" | grep -c 'FAIL')
P2_PASS=$(echo "$PHASE2" | grep -c 'PASS')
P2_FAIL=$(echo "$PHASE2" | grep -c 'FAIL')
quickRule: If parse_results.sh contains 'node' or 'python', it's a template violation.

title: The Triangle: Prompt ↔ Rubric ↔ Tests Must Align
reminderTitle: Every requirement needs a rubric criterion AND an F2P test. Both, independently.
reminderBody:
The three core artifacts must form a closed triangle:
- Every prompt requirement → rubric criterion + F2P test + gold patch change
- Every rubric criterion → at least one test that would catch a violation
- Every F2P test → a prompt requirement it traces to
- Gold patch → implements every requirement, nothing more

Common alignment gaps:
- Rubric has criterion for X, but no F2P test checks X → model implements wrong and still passes
- F2P test checks X, but no rubric criterion covers X → test result doesn't affect the score
- Gold patch implements Y, but Y isn't in the prompt → orphan implementation

Before submitting: R1 → criterion ✓ → F2P test ✓ → gold patch change ✓
badExample:
Prompt requires: (1) /api/cart endpoint, (2) 401 for unauthenticated requests

Rubric: endpoint exists ✅ | 401 behavior [MISSING]
F2P tests: endpoint test ✅ | 401 test [MISSING]

→ Model skips authentication and passes QC
goodExample:
Prompt requires: (1) /api/cart endpoint, (2) 401 for unauthenticated requests

Rubric: endpoint exists ✅ | unauthenticated returns 401 ✅
F2P tests: GET /api/cart → 200 ✅ | GET /api/cart without token → 401 ✅
Gold patch: implements both ✅

→ Full coverage, nothing slips through
quickRule: For every requirement: rubric criterion ✓ + F2P test ✓ + gold patch change ✓. All three or it's a gap.

title: Model Failure Validity Not All Failures Are Equal
reminderTitle: The model must be ABLE to solve the task just not reliably.
reminderBody:
A valid model failure means: a capable model attempts the task in good faith and applies a reasonable solution, but the solution is incomplete or incorrect in ways detectable by the rubric and tests.

Invalid failures (task gets rejected):
- The prompt is impossible to implement correctly
- The codebase is broken before any patch is applied
- The model succeeds 100% of the time (task is too easy)
- The model fails because the prompt is too vague to act on
- The task requires knowledge no model could have

Valid failures:
- Model implements the feature but misses an edge case
- Model implements the happy path but not the error handling
- Model uses a correct approach but doesn't satisfy all rubric criteria
quickRule: If no model can succeed, the task is invalid. If every model always succeeds, it's too easy. Aim for the middle.
